topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The late afternoon sun slanted through the thinning canopy, painting stripes of gold across the damp earth. Elias dragged himself across the patchy lawn, his joints protesting with every movement. He stopped by the spot where the sapling had stood—a spindly thing he’d nurtured with obsessive care during his childhood summers. Now, only stumps remained, jagged reminders of a future that had been neatly erased. He knelt, brushing away the clinging soil, his fingers tracing the raw, exposed wood. A heavy, dull ache settled in his chest, a familiar, heavy weight. He didn't cry; the tears seemed too extravagant an expenditure of energy. He just stared at the empty space, the silence of the cleared ground pressing down on him until his breath hitched, shallow and ragged.

[story 2]

Sarah leaned against the fence, the wood cool and rough beneath her palms. She hadn't slept properly in days, the exhaustion clinging to her like a shroud. It was the sight of the barren patch that finally broke the dam of her composure. That small oak, planted with earnest, hopeful hands when she was barely tall enough to reach the soil, was gone. She traced the line where the roots had been, a phantom sensation of life beneath the surface. A slow, shaky exhale escaped her lips. "Gone," she whispered to the indifferent sky. The effort of standing up felt monumental, a deliberate act of resistance against the profound depletion settling deep in her bones.

[story 3]

Mark stood at the edge of the clearing, his shoulders slumped forward, his gaze fixed on the bare ground. The effort of simply observing the scene seemed to drain the last reserves of his energy. He remembered the vibrant green of that young tree, a symbol of unwavering commitment from his youth. Now, there was only stark, exposed earth where life had once flourished. A low groan escaped him, a sound more from his core than his throat. He didn't move to touch anything; the sheer inertia of his fatigue kept him rooted, a statue carved from sheer depletion, watching the finality of the cut wood.
